overall_assessment: Compose stays on-subject, ranks all five named options against the brief's five axes (first-run success, maintenance, release/versioning, host compatibility, long-term product shape), and lands on a phased recommendation that is internally consistent with its own ranking. Epistemic calibration is appropriate: the answer explicitly flags that evidence is thin, separates the brief-confirmed facts from assumptions about Claude Code's Node prerequisite and circuit-next packaging, and names four concrete proofs (package build check, cross-platform shell-out check, clean-machine first-run script, versioning policy doc) that would move the recommendation from assumption-grounded to evidence-grounded. evidence_refs cite only reports/brief.json and reports/analysis.json, which are the two real artifacts provided in this run; nothing fabricated. success_condition_alignment is substantive — it names the five comparison axes, the assumption/fact split, and the four specific proofs, so it would not paste cleanly into an unrelated compose.
missed_angles: Licensing/distribution implications of publishing circuit-next to npm (package name squatting, org ownership, public vs. scoped) are not weighed, which can constrain the npm-as-canonical-channel recommendation.
Offline/air-gapped and corporate-proxy install scenarios are not considered, which materially affect whether npm-global is a viable default channel for some Claude Code users.

No discussion of how the plugin detects an outdated (vs. merely missing) circuit-next and whether the version-skew prompt is silent on match, which is the part of the detect-and-prompt UX most likely to regress first-run success.
